content_markdown:
# Getting an ABN as a Locum Doctor: A Step-by-Step Guide
*[5 min read | Locum Pay & Rates | May 2026]*

---

**TL;DR:** Locum doctors in Australia need an Australian Business Number (ABN) to work as independent contractors. This guide outlines the steps to register for an ABN, including choosing your business structure and confirming GST registration. Avoid common pitfalls like using your ABN for non-locum work. Learn more at [Australian Business Register](https://abr.gov.au).

---

> [KEY FACTS]
> - An ABN is a unique 11-digit number identifying your business.
> - Locum doctors need an ABN to invoice directly and manage taxes.
> - GST registration is required if annual income exceeds A$75,000.

## What is an ABN?

An Australian Business Number (ABN) is a unique 11-digit identifier for your business. For locum doctors, having an ABN signifies that you are operating as a sole trader or through a company, rather than as an employee. This distinction is crucial for managing your business dealings with hospitals and clinics.

The ABN is essential for invoicing and tax purposes. It allows you to be recognised by the government and other businesses, ensuring that your professional activities are properly documented and compliant with Australian regulations.

> [BIG STAT] **11-digit** unique identifier for businesses, Australian Business Register (2026)

## Why do locum doctors need an ABN?

Locum doctors require an ABN for several reasons. It allows direct invoicing for services rendered, providing control over income and expenses. This flexibility is ideal for doctors who wish to work with multiple hospitals or clinics without being tied to a single employer.

Without an ABN, locum doctors might be subject to PAYG employment, which can restrict flexibility and potentially lead to higher tax withholdings. Therefore, obtaining an ABN is a critical step for any doctor considering locum work.

> [INSIGHT: 💡 | Tax Planning | Setting aside funds for tax and superannuation is crucial for locum doctors.]

## How do you get an ABN as a locum doctor?

### Step 1: Visit the Australian Business Register (ABR)

Begin by visiting the official Australian Business Register website at abr.gov.au. Click on "Apply for an ABN" and follow the online prompts to start your application.

### Step 2: Choose Your Business Structure

Most locum doctors find that registering as a sole trader is the simplest option. However, you can opt to set up a company (Pty Ltd) if you plan to expand or split income for tax purposes in the future.

### Step 3: Enter Your Details

You'll need to provide your Tax File Number (TFN), full name, address, and business activity (select "medical services" or "locum doctor"). Also, indicate the date you started or plan to start your locum work.

### Step 4: Confirm GST Registration

If your annual income is expected to exceed A$75,000, you must register for GST. This involves adding 10% GST to your invoices and submitting a Business Activity Statement (BAS) quarterly to pay this tax. It's advisable to register for GST upfront to streamline your financial management.

### Step 5: Submit and Record Your ABN

Upon approval, you'll receive your ABN instantly. Keep this number secure and include it on all invoices sent to hospitals or agencies.

> [KEY TAKEAWAY] Registering for GST upfront simplifies financial management for locum doctors.

## What does this mean for locum doctors in New South Wales?

For locum doctors in New South Wales, obtaining an ABN is a straightforward process that opens doors to numerous opportunities. With an ABN, doctors can work across various hospitals and clinics in the state, enjoying the flexibility and independence that locum work offers.

New South Wales has a high demand for locum doctors, particularly in rural and regional areas. An ABN allows you to meet this demand while managing your own tax and superannuation obligations. This autonomy can lead to greater job satisfaction and financial rewards.

> [KEY TAKEAWAY] An ABN provides locum doctors in New South Wales with flexibility and independence in their professional practice.

## Frequently Asked Questions

**Q: What happens if I don't get an ABN?**  
A: Without an ABN, you may be treated as an employee under PAYG arrangements, which can limit your flexibility and result in higher tax withholdings. It also restricts your ability to invoice directly and manage your own business expenses.

**Q: Can I apply for an ABN if I'm not yet working as a locum?**  
A: Yes, you can apply for an ABN in anticipation of starting locum work. During the application, you'll need to provide the date you plan to start your locum activities.

**Q: Is it mandatory to register for GST if my income is below A$75,000?**  
A: No, GST registration is only mandatory if your annual income exceeds A$75,000. However, registering for GST can simplify your invoicing and tax processes from the outset.

**Q: Can I use my ABN for other types of work?**  
A: It's advisable to use your ABN specifically for locum work to avoid tax complications. Mixing business activities under one ABN can lead to compliance issues.

**Q: How do I manage my tax and super as a locum doctor?**  
A: As a contractor, you are responsible for setting aside funds for tax and superannuation. It's important to plan your finances carefully to meet these obligations.

**Q: What should I do if I exceed the GST threshold after obtaining my ABN?**  
A: If your income surpasses the A$75,000 threshold, you must register for GST. This involves updating your ABN details and submitting regular BAS to the Australian Taxation Office.

For more guidance on managing your locum career, visit [StatDoctor](https://statdoctor.app).

> **Disclaimer:** This content is for general information purposes only and does not constitute medical, legal, or financial advice. Always consult a qualified professional for advice specific to your situation.

> **Note on pay rates:** Figures mentioned are indicative only and vary by location, specialty, employer, and individual enterprise agreement.

## Sources

1. [Australian Business Register](https://abr.gov.au), Australian Government
2. [Australian Taxation Office](https://www.ato.gov.au), Australian Government
